questions: Within the statewide poison management network that serves California, several physicians act as division-level medical directors. Identify two physicians who currently serve as medical directors within this system and compare their educational backgrounds, academic appointments, and primary clinical or research interests in toxicology. Your answer should synthesize information from institutional profiles, university affiliations, and professional biographies.

Within California’s statewide poison control network, several physician leaders serve as medical directors of different regional divisions. Identify two physicians who currently hold medical director roles in separate divisions of this system. For each physician, describe (1) their medical specialty and board certifications, (2) their primary academic or hospital appointment, and (3) their specific leadership role within the poison control network, including the region or division they oversee. Compare how their clinical backgrounds align with the mission of a poison control service.

Within the field of healthcare professionals in the United States, identify the statewide organization in California that provides 24/7 expert consultation and toxicology support specifically to medical providers managing poisoning or exposure cases. Describe its core services for healthcare professionals, its organizational structure or academic affiliations, and how it is funded or supported at the state and national levels.

Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) operates as a statewide poison management network divided into several regional divisions, each overseen by physician medical directors. Two prominent medical directors within this system are **Dr. Craig Smollin** and **Dr. Cyrus Rangan**.

**Dr. Craig Smollin** serves as the Medical Director of the San Francisco Division of CPCS [1]. He earned his medical degree from the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) and completed emergency medicine residency and a medical toxicology fellowship [1]. He holds an academic appointment as a Professor of Emergency Medicine at UCSF School of Medicine [1]. His primary clinical and research interests center on emergency toxicology, envenomation management, drug overdose treatment, and the intersection of toxicology with public health. He is actively involved in teaching medical students and residents in both emergency medicine and clinical toxicology [1].

**Dr. Cyrus Rangan** serves as the Medical Director associated with the CPCS and also holds the position of Director of the Toxicology and Environmental Assessment Bureau at the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health [2]. He received his medical degree from the Chicago Medical School and completed training in pediatrics as well as a fellowship in medical toxicology [2]. His academic appointments include a clinical faculty position affiliated with the University of California system. Dr. Rangan's primary interests focus on pediatric toxicology, environmental health, lead poisoning prevention, and public health toxicology, particularly assessing chemical exposures in community settings [2].

Comparing the two, Dr. Smollin's background emphasizes emergency medicine and acute clinical toxicology within an academic medical center, while Dr. Rangan integrates pediatric medicine with environmental and public health-oriented toxicology. Both hold university-affiliated teaching roles and contribute to poison management policy, but their subspecialty emphases—acute care versus environmental and pediatric toxicology—reflect complementary expertise within CPCS.

## References

[1] University of California, San Francisco. "Craig Smollin, MD – Faculty Profile." UCSF Department of Emergency Medicine. https://emergency.ucsf.edu/people/craig-smollin

[2] Los Angeles County Department of Public Health. "Cyrus Rangan, MD – Toxicology and Environmental Assessment." http://publichealth.lacounty.gov/

[3] California Poison Control System. "About CPCS – Leadership and Divisions." https://calpoison.org
rounds.answer_b:
Shideh Pouria, MD, is a medical director within the California Poison Control System (CPCS) and Assistant Professor of Emergency Medicine at UC San Diego. She completed medical school at the University of Rochester, then an emergency medicine residency at UC San Diego and a medical toxicology fellowship at the Toxikon Consortium in Chicago.[1][2] Her academic appointment is in Emergency Medicine, with clinical roles in both emergency and inpatient toxicology consultation. Her interests include acute poisoning management, envenomations, and medical education in toxicology, reflected in her work supervising fellows and residents and contributing to toxicology curricula.[1][2]

Craig Smollin, MD, serves as Medical Director of the San Francisco Division of CPCS and is a Professor of Emergency Medicine at UCSF.[3][4] He obtained his MD from Jefferson Medical College, completed emergency medicine residency at Brown University, and a medical toxicology fellowship at UCSF.[3] His academic role is more senior, involving leadership in the UCSF Division of Medical Toxicology and direction of the UCSF medical toxicology fellowship.[3][4] Smollin’s primary interests include clinical toxicology, antidote use, and outcomes research in poisoning, as well as systems-level toxicology practice and education.[3][4]

In comparison, both are emergency physicians and fellowship-trained medical toxicologists working as CPCS medical directors and academic faculty. Pouria’s profile emphasizes clinical toxicology care and education, whereas Smollin’s adds a larger focus on fellowship leadership and research in toxicology outcomes and systems of care.[1–4]

## References  
[1] UC San Diego Department of Emergency Medicine – Shideh Pouria, MD profile.  
[2] California Poison Control System – UC San Diego division leadership/biography pages.  
[3] UCSF Department of Emergency Medicine – Craig Smollin, MD faculty profile.  
[4] California Poison Control System – San Francisco Division / UCSF toxicology leadership biographies.

Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) is a statewide network administered through the University of California, San Francisco, School of Pharmacy and organized into four regional divisions, each led by a physician medical director [1].

**Dr. Craig Smollin** serves as the Medical Director of the CPCS San Francisco Division [1][2]. He is board-certified in both emergency medicine and medical toxicology [2]. Dr. Smollin holds an appointment as a Professor of Clinical Emergency Medicine at the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF), where he practices at Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital [2]. His clinical expertise spans acute poisoning management, drug overdose, and envenomation. He is also actively involved in training emergency medicine residents and medical toxicology fellows at UCSF [2]. His dual board certification equips him to manage the acute, time-sensitive cases that are central to poison control operations.

**Dr. Justin Lewis** serves as the Medical Director of the CPCS Fresno/Madera Division [1][3]. He is board-certified in emergency medicine and medical toxicology [3]. Dr. Lewis holds an academic appointment at UCSF Fresno, where he practices emergency medicine and serves as a clinical faculty member [3]. His interests include agricultural and occupational chemical exposures, which are particularly relevant to the Central Valley region his division serves [3]. His background in emergency medicine combined with toxicology training aligns directly with the demands of managing pesticide and chemical exposure calls common in an agricultural region.

Both physicians share dual board certification in emergency medicine and medical toxicology, reflecting the CPCS model of staffing divisions with experts who can provide real-time clinical guidance on acute poisoning emergencies [1]. However, their regional contexts shape their focus areas: Dr. Smollin's urban San Francisco setting emphasizes drug overdose and substance-related toxicology, while Dr. Lewis's Central Valley location demands expertise in agricultural and environmental chemical exposures. Together, their complementary backgrounds demonstrate how CPCS tailors physician leadership to meet regional needs while maintaining consistent statewide expertise.

## References

[1] California Poison Control System. "About CPCS." https://calpoison.org

[2] University of California, San Francisco. "Craig Smollin, MD – Faculty Profile." UCSF Department of Emergency Medicine. https://emergency.ucsf.edu/people/craig-smollin

[3] UCSF Fresno. "Emergency Medicine and Toxicology Faculty." https://www.fresno.ucsf.edu/em/
rounds.answer_b:
Within the California Poison Control System (CPCS), physician medical directors lead regional divisions that provide 24/7 toxicology expertise and public health surveillance.

**1. Craig Smollin, MD – San Francisco Division**

1) Medical specialty/board certifications: Dr. Smollin is an emergency physician, board-certified in Emergency Medicine and in Medical Toxicology.[1][2]  
2) Academic/hospital appointment: He is a Professor of Emergency Medicine at the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) and an attending physician in the UCSF Emergency Department.[1][2]  
3) Poison control leadership role: Dr. Smollin serves as the Medical Director of the San Francisco Division of the CPCS and directs the UCSF Medical Toxicology Fellowship, overseeing toxicology consultation services and poison center activities for the Northern California region.[1][2]

**2. Shideh Pouria, MD – San Diego Division**

1) Medical specialty/board certifications: Dr. Pouria is an emergency physician, board-certified in Emergency Medicine and fellowship‑trained in Medical Toxicology.[3][4]  
2) Academic/hospital appointment: She is an Assistant Professor of Emergency Medicine at the University of California, San Diego (UC San Diego) and practices clinically in the UC San Diego Health emergency departments and on the inpatient toxicology consult service.[3]  
3) Poison control leadership role: Dr. Pouria is a Medical Director within the San Diego Division of the CPCS, providing oversight for regional poison center operations and supervising trainees in clinical toxicology.[3][4]

**Comparison and alignment with poison control mission**

Both physicians are emergency medicine–trained and toxicology‑focused, matching the CPCS mission of rapid assessment and management of acute poisonings, overdoses, and envenomations. Their dual roles—as frontline emergency clinicians and academic toxicologists—support evidence‑based triage, antidote use, and consultation for both community callers and hospitals. Smollin’s senior academic rank and fellowship leadership emphasize systems-level toxicology practice and research, whereas Pouria’s role is more centered on direct clinical care and education in toxicology; together, these complementary backgrounds strengthen CPCS’s clinical quality, training, and regional outreach.[1–4]

## References  
[1] UCSF Department of Emergency Medicine – Craig Smollin, MD faculty profile.  
[2] California Poison Control System – San Francisco Division leadership pages.  
[3] UC San Diego Department of Emergency Medicine – Shideh Pouria, MD profile.  
[4] California Poison Control System – San Diego/UCSD division leadership biographies.

However, because they selected the wrong physicians relative to the required checklist, they fail the WIDTH criteria tied to the specified individuals.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both responses are well-formatted, scannable, and professionally written, with clear headings and comparative analysis. However, since both fail the foundational entity requirement, accuracy overrides formatting quality.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The **California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is the statewide organization in California that provides 24/7 expert consultation and toxicology support specifically to healthcare professionals managing poisoning or exposure cases [1][2].

**Core Services for Healthcare Professionals:**
CPCS offers round-the-clock telephone consultation staffed by specially trained pharmacists, physicians, and nurses who are certified specialists in poison information. Healthcare providers can access real-time guidance on the identification, assessment, and management of poisoning and toxic exposure cases [1]. Services include treatment recommendations for drug overdoses, chemical exposures, envenomations, and hazardous materials incidents. CPCS also provides antidote consultation, drug interaction assessments, and clinical toxicology follow-up services. The system handles approximately 300,000 calls annually and serves as a critical resource for emergency departments, primary care providers, and pre-hospital personnel across California [2][3].

**Organizational Structure and Academic Affiliations:**
CPCS is a division of the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy**, Department of Clinical Pharmacy [1][2]. It operates through four regional centers strategically located in San Francisco, Sacramento, San Diego, and Fresno, which collectively provide seamless statewide coverage. This academic affiliation ensures that CPCS integrates clinical toxicology research, education, and training into its operations [3].

**Funding and Support:**
CPCS receives funding through a combination of state appropriations from the California State Legislature and federal support through the **Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA)** Poison Center Grant Program [2][4]. Additional funding comes from UCSF and various grants supporting toxicology research and surveillance activities.

## References
1. California Poison Control System. "About CPCS." https://calpoison.org
2. University of California, San Francisco. "California Poison Control System." https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/cpcs
3. American Association of Poison Control Centers. "Find Your Local Poison Center." https://aapcc.org
4. Health Resources and Services Administration. "Poison Control Program." https://www.hrsa.gov/poison-control
rounds.answer_b:
The primary statewide organization in California that provides 24/7 expert toxicology consultation to medical providers is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**. CPCS operates a round‑the‑clock toll‑free line staffed by specially trained pharmacists, nurses, and board‑certified medical and clinical toxicologists who advise physicians, emergency departments, EMS, ICU teams, and other clinicians on diagnosis, risk assessment, decontamination, antidote use, and monitoring of poisoned or exposed patients.[1][2]  

For healthcare professionals, CPCS offers real‑time bedside management consultation, assistance with complex or rare toxicologic syndromes, guidance on drug–drug or chemical interactions, occupational and environmental exposures, and support during mass‑exposure or disaster events.[1][3] It also develops clinical guidelines, provides education programs (lectures, rotations, and fellowships in medical toxicology), and participates in surveillance and research on poisonings statewide.[1][3]  

Organizationally, CPCS is administered by the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy**, with its regional poison control centers located at academic medical centers in San Francisco, Sacramento, Fresno, and San Diego.[1][2] It is part of the national network of U.S. poison centers coordinated with the American Association of Poison Control Centers (AAPCC).[4]  

Funding is mixed: CPCS is supported by the **California Department of Public Health and Emergency Medical Services Authority**, federal support via the **Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA)** and other HHS mechanisms, UCSF institutional support, and competitive grants and contracts.[1][3][4]  

## References  
[1] California Poison Control System – About & Services  
[2] UCSF School of Pharmacy – California Poison Control System overview  
[3] California Poison Control System – Healthcare professionals/education pages  
[4] American Association of Poison Control Centers – Member center listing and funding overview
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both Agent A and Agent B correctly identify the California Poison Control System (CPCS), passing the DEEP logic check. Both accurately describe 24/7 consultation services, expert toxicology support, statewide structure with four centers, UCSF affiliation, and mixed state/federal funding (including HRSA), satisfying the WIDTH checklist.

However, Agent A provides superior user experience through clearer structure and formatting. It uses bold section headers and bullet-style segmentation that make the response more scannable and aligned with search-engine-style presentation. The organization (Core Services → Structure → Funding) is especially clean and easy to navigate. Agent B is comprehensive and accurate but presented in denser paragraph form, making it slightly less immediately digestible.

Since both are factually strong and complete, the decision is based on presentation and usability. Agent A wins on formatting and scannability, with no substantive errors on either side.
